news_summary: A leading climate center is actively holding the Trump administration accountable through legal action to protect the environment.
news_summary_fr: Un centre de référence en matière de climat engage activement l'administration Trump en justice afin de protéger l'environnement.
news_summary_es: Un importante centro dedicado al clima está exigiendo responsabilidades a la Administración Trump mediante acciones legales para proteger el medio ambiente.

articles.title: World-leading climate centre takes Trump administration to court
articles.summary: Nature, Published online: 08 May 2026; doi:10.1038/d41586-026-01501-zUniversities that run the National Center for Atmospheric Research want to keep it from being dismantled.
articles.content:
Denver, Colorado

In one of the highest-profile battles yet between the US research community and the administration of President Donald Trump, lawyers faced off in a Colorado courthouse yesterday over the future of a research centre that has been called the global ‘mothership’ of climate science.

Trump team plans to break up ‘global mothership’ of climate science

Under Trump, the US government has said that it will take steps to dismantle the National Center for Atmospheric Research (NCAR) in Boulder, Colorado, alleging that it promotes climate alarmism. The organization that manages NCAR — a coalition of around 130 universities called the University Corporation for Atmospheric Research (UCAR) — sued the government in March to stop NCAR’s break-up.

Researchers say that NCAR is a crucial global resource whose models underpin much of modern atmospheric science, including artificial-intelligence studies1 aimed at decoding and predicting extreme weather. “Losing NCAR would mean losing decades of institutional knowledge, something that cannot be plugged back in two or four or even ten years from now,” says Angeline Pendergrass, an atmospheric scientist at Cornell University in Ithaca, New York.

Legal action

At the heart of the legal fight is whether the US National Science Foundation (NSF), which provides the bulk of NCAR’s funding through a contract with UCAR, is moving too quickly and without authority to hand off pieces of NCAR — including a supercomputing centre in Cheyenne, Wyoming — to public and private institutions.

A lawyer representing the NSF argued in court that no final decisions had been taken; an NSF spokesperson told Nature that he had nothing further to add.

Massive budget cuts for US science proposed again by Trump administration

Documents released as part of the UCAR lawsuit show that last November, the White House’s budget office told the NSF to begin restructuring NCAR to align the centre’s mission “more closely with Administration priorities”. The news became public in December. By January, the NSF asked for proposals on how NCAR should be reorganized, including a request for public comments by 13 March. But the documents show that well before that deadline arrived, on 12 February, the NSF told NCAR officials that the agency had already decided to transfer the stewardship of its supercomputing centre elsewhere. “That’s remarkably fast for a consequential decision such as this,” says Carlos Javier Martinez, a climate scientist at the Union of Concerned Scientists in Cambridge, Massachusetts.

On 3 April, UCAR asked a US district court judge to freeze the plans to give away the supercomputing centre. “This is a sham process,” Michael Purpura, a solicitor at the Hueston Hennigan law firm in Newport Beach, California, argued on UCAR’s behalf at the 7 May court hearing.

NCAR’s iconic Mesa Lab in Boulder, Colorado, was designed by the architect I. M. Pei.

Representing the NSF, solicitor Marianne Kies argued that despite the actions taken so far, the agency had not made any decisions on the future of the supercomputing centre or the rest of NCAR. “A final decision has not been made to transfer stewardship,” said Kies, who works with the US Department of Justice in Washington DC. “It’s not the case that NSF failed to follow procedure — it’s just that the need to follow procedure has not been triggered yet.”

The judge, R. Brooke Jackson of the US District Court of Colorado, told both sides that he would issue a decision “as promptly as possible”. If he finds in favour of the NSF, the process of handing off the supercomputing centre will carry on. If he finds in favour of UCAR, that transfer is likely to be put on hold until the parties work out some other arrangement. Whatever the ruling, the broader battle over the future of NCAR — including its aeroplane fleet, space-weather studies and climate-modelling teams — will probably continue to play out.

Scientists and other people who use NCAR data could see “ripple effects” caused by the current uncertainty, including on weather forecasts and the people who rely on them, says Amanda Staudt, executive director of the American Meteorological Society in Boston, Massachusetts. “Major changes like those proposed by NSF can be very disruptive.”
articles.reasoning: The article describes a significant legal action taken by a coalition of universities to protect the National Center for Atmospheric Research (NCAR), a crucial global climate science resource, from being dismantled by the US government. This fight preserves an institution vital for climate research, weather prediction, and AI studies related to atmospheric science, which has broad societal and environmental benefits. The story is focused, detailed, and highlights the importance of maintaining scientific infrastructure for climate action and public welfare.
articles.personality_title: Universities fight to protect key US climate research centre from closure
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – The National Center for Atmospheric Research (NCAR) in Boulder, Colorado, is a leading climate science facility supported by a group of about 130 universities called the University Corporation for Atmospheric Research (UCAR). It provides important climate models and weather data used worldwide.

**What happened** – In 2026, the US government under President Donald Trump planned to break up NCAR, claiming it promotes climate alarmism. UCAR sued the government to stop this. A court hearing took place in Colorado where UCAR asked to freeze plans to transfer NCAR’s supercomputing centre to other institutions. The government argued no final decisions had been made yet.

**Impact** – NCAR holds decades of climate knowledge and runs supercomputers that help predict extreme weather and study the atmosphere using artificial intelligence. Losing or breaking up the centre could disrupt weather forecasts and scientific research that many people and communities rely on.

**What’s next step** – The court judge will decide soon whether to pause the transfer of NCAR’s supercomputing centre. Regardless of the ruling, the fight over NCAR’s future will likely continue as the government and scientists work out what happens next.

**One-sentence takeaway** – A coalition of universities is taking legal action to protect a vital US climate research centre from government plans to dismantle it, aiming to preserve important tools for weather prediction and climate science.
articles.personality_title_fr: Des universités défendent un centre clé de recherche climatique américain contre sa fermeture
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Le Centre national de recherche atmosphérique (NCAR) à Boulder, Colorado, est une installation majeure en sciences du climat soutenue par environ 130 universités regroupées dans la University Corporation for Atmospheric Research (UCAR). Il fournit des modèles climatiques et des données météorologiques importants utilisés dans le monde entier.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – En 2026, le gouvernement américain sous la présidence de Donald Trump a prévu de démanteler le NCAR, l’accusant de promouvoir l’alarmisme climatique. L’UCAR a porté plainte pour empêcher cela. Une audience a eu lieu dans un tribunal du Colorado où l’UCAR a demandé de geler les plans de transfert du centre de supercalculateurs du NCAR à d’autres institutions. Le gouvernement a affirmé qu’aucune décision finale n’avait encore été prise.

**Impact** – Le NCAR possède des décennies de connaissances climatiques et exploite des supercalculateurs qui aident à prévoir les phénomènes météorologiques extrêmes et à étudier l’atmosphère grâce à l’intelligence artificielle. Perdre ou démanteler ce centre pourrait perturber les prévisions météorologiques et la recherche scientifique dont dépendent de nombreuses personnes et communautés.

**Prochaine étape** – Le juge du tribunal doit bientôt décider s’il suspend le transfert du centre de supercalculateurs du NCAR. Quel que soit le verdict, le débat sur l’avenir du NCAR devrait continuer alors que le gouvernement et les scientifiques cherchent une solution.

**Résumé en une phrase** – Un groupe d’universités engage une action en justice pour protéger un centre américain crucial de recherche climatique contre des projets gouvernementaux de démantèlement, afin de préserver des outils essentiels à la prévision météo et à la science du climat.
articles.personality_title_es: Universidades luchan para proteger centro clave de investigación climática en EE.UU. de su cierre
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – El Centro Nacional de Investigación Atmosférica (NCAR) en Boulder, Colorado, es una instalación líder en ciencia climática apoyada por unas 130 universidades agrupadas en la University Corporation for Atmospheric Research (UCAR). Proporciona modelos climáticos y datos meteorológicos importantes usados en todo el mundo.

**Qué pasó** – En 2026, el gobierno de Estados Unidos bajo el presidente Donald Trump planeó desmantelar NCAR, alegando que promueve el alarmismo climático. UCAR demandó al gobierno para detener esta acción. En una audiencia judicial en Colorado, UCAR pidió detener los planes para transferir el centro de supercomputación de NCAR a otras instituciones. El gobierno dijo que no se habían tomado decisiones finales.

**Impacto** – NCAR tiene décadas de conocimiento climático y opera supercomputadoras que ayudan a predecir fenómenos meteorológicos extremos y a estudiar la atmósfera usando inteligencia artificial. Perder o dividir el centro podría afectar las predicciones del tiempo y la investigación científica que muchas personas y comunidades necesitan.

**Próximo paso** – El juez decidirá pronto si pausa la transferencia del centro de supercomputación de NCAR. Sin importar el fallo, la disputa sobre el futuro del NCAR probablemente continuará mientras el gobierno y los científicos buscan una solución.

**Conclusión en una frase** – Un grupo de universidades está tomando acciones legales para proteger un centro clave de investigación climática en EE.UU. frente a planes gubernamentales de desmantelamiento, buscando conservar herramientas esenciales para la predicción del clima y la ciencia atmosférica.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed, warm-toned painting of a sturdy, modern mountain-top research lab with large windows glowing softly at dusk, surrounded by swirling clouds shaped like digital data streams and weather patterns, while a protective, translucent shield gently encircles the building symbolizing legal defense and preservation of climate science.
